title: Poles-zeros duality in semi-holographic Mott insulators
authors: Thomas Kögel, Alessio Caddeo, Amelie Pitters, Francesca Paoletti, Lorenzo Crippa, Giorgio Sangiovanni, René Meyer, Johanna Erdmenger
abstract: Inspired by the poles-zeros duality of Green's functions that appears in transitions into Mott-insulating phases in strongly correlated condensed matter systems, we propose a semi-holographic approach to Mott insulators. In this model, a fundamental fermion is coupled to a large-$N$, strongly interacting sector that generates a self-energy for the fundamental fermion's Green's function. This coupling amounts to a hybridization of the fundamental fermion with a strongly correlated fermionic composite. Within the holographic framework, at large $N$, the Green's function of the composite fermion naturally exhibits a poles-zeros duality. Zeros of the Green's function are caused by the poles of the self-energy that correspond to collective many-body excitations of the holographic strongly interacting sector. We calculate the spectral function of the fundamental fermion, from which we characterize the semi-holographic metallic and the Mott-insulating phases. In addition to the new physical interpretation of the zeros, our analysis yields a well-defined picture of the poles-zeros duality in terms of the freedom to choose between standard and alternative quantization in the strongly coupled sector.
summary: This paper uses a semi-holographic framework to explain the poles-zeros duality observed in the transition to Mott-insulating phases. It shows that the zeros in the Green's function (Luttinger surfaces) arise from the poles of the self-energy, which are linked to collective excitations in a strongly coupled sector. The study provides a clear mapping between the insulating and metallic phases through the choice of holographic quantization.
main_problem: Understanding the physical mechanism behind the poles-zeros duality in the Green's functions of Mott insulators and the emergence of Luttinger surfaces.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a poles-zeros duality in the self-energy, driven by a bulk scalar coupling, induces an approximate duality in the single-particle Green's function, effectively mapping the Mott-insulating phase to a metallic phase.
method: A semi-holographic approach using the AdS/CFT correspondence, involving the calculation of retarded Green's functions in an AdS Reissner-Nordstrom black hole background and analyzing the transition between standard and alternative quantizations.
model_or_system: A semi-holographic model consisting of a fundamental Dirac fermion coupled to a strongly interacting large-N sector, where the latter is described by a 3+1D gravitational theory with a non-minimal scalar coupling.
key_observables: Retarded Green's function (G_R), self-energy (Sigma_R), spectral function (rho), and the determinant of the Green's function.
important_parameters: Non-minimal scalar coupling parameter (eta), large-N limit, chemical potential (mu), and temperature (T).
main_assumptions: The large-N limit is used to treat the strongly coupled sector as a bath and neglect backreaction on the gravitational background; the probe approximation is applied to the bulk fermion.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the self-energy magnitude; Figure 2 compares the determinant of the Green's function; Figure 3 shows the determinant at k=0 identifying poles and zeros; Figure 4 relates the determinant to the self-energy; Figure 5 shows the Mott gap size vs eta; Figures 6 and 7 show the real and imaginary parts of the Green's function in the insulating and metallic regimes, respectively.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Green's function zeros, defines the semi-holographic model and its coupling, derives the analytical poles-zeros duality for the self-energy via quantization schemes, performs numerical analysis of the spectral functions and phase transitions, and discusses the physical implications and limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2: Comparison of log | det GR(ω, k, η)| for opposite-sign values of the bulk coupling, η = 5 (metallic, in panel (a)) and η = −5 (Mott insulating, in panel (b)). Bright yellow regions indicate peaks in the quantity, while dark purple regions correspond to zeros. The horizontal dashed lines represent the Fermi level which is ωF ≈−1.2 for the metallic phase (left) while it is fixed in the middle of the gap, i.e. at ωF ≈−1.3 in the Mott insulator (right). The poles and zeros of | det GR(ω, k, η)| exhibit an approximate one-to-one correspondence over the displayed range of momenta and frequencies.

Figure 5: For negative values of η, the system is a Mott insulator and here we plot its gap size as a function of η < 0. The gap opens at η = −0.17 for the cutoff 10−2 and at η = −0.51 for the 10−5 cutoff. The datapoints are determined from the average of the result for the gap for the two different cutoffs, while the error bars are given by the deviation from the average. For larger negative values of η the two criteria converge towards the same value due to the spectral features becoming sharper.
